_purpose: Local economy timeline for the TX East Piney Woods regional cluster (Nacogdoches, Angelina, Cherokee, Smith, Rusk, Harrison, Marion, Cass counties). Covers 1820-1960 across six economic eras — from Southern migration frontier through the East Texas Oil Field. Designed to give Polsia the economic backdrop for any ancestor living in this eight-county region.
display_name: TX East Piney Woods — Local Economy Timeline

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Subsistence farming transitioning to cotton; Republic of Texas land grants; Cherokee agricultural economy (until 1839 expulsion)
economic_eras.description: East Texas was the terminus of Southern migration — families from Alabama, Mississippi, Georgia, Tennessee, and the Carolinas came for cheap land under Mexican colonization grants and later Republic of Texas headrights. The Cherokee and associated bands (Delaware, Shawnee, Kickapoo) maintained a parallel agricultural economy in the northern counties until their expulsion in 1839. Cotton was the cash crop where soil permitted, but the early economy was primarily subsistence — corn, hogs, sweet potatoes, and garden crops. The steamboat route through Caddo Lake and Big Cypress Bayou connected Harrison and Marion counties to Shreveport and New Orleans, giving those counties a commercial advantage over the landlocked interior.

county_specifics.note: Oldest Anglo settlement in Texas. El Camino Real passed through the town. The Stone Fort was the commercial center. Small-scale cotton farming on sandy loam. Republic-era land grants attracted Southern families.
Rich soil and Caddo Lake steamboat access made Harrison County the first in East Texas to develop commercial cotton agriculture. By 1845, large slaveholding operations were already established.
Jefferson's steamboat landing on Big Cypress Bayou was the gateway to East Texas. The town grew rapidly as the entry point for goods and immigrants.
Cherokee and associated bands occupied the northern portion until the Cherokee War of 1839. Anglo settlers moved onto Cherokee improvements immediately after the expulsion.
Organized in 1846 after the Cherokee expulsion opened the land. Tyler was established as the county seat. Early economy was subsistence farming.
Organized in 1843. Henderson was the county seat. Subsistence farming with some cotton on the sandy loam uplands.
Organized in 1846. Sparsely settled — dense pine forest with scattered subsistence farms along creek bottoms.
Northern county near the Arkansas border. Subsistence farming with some cotton. Iron ore deposits would later support furnace operations.

key_infrastructure.name: El Camino Real (King's Highway)
key_infrastructure.impact: The Spanish road from Nacogdoches to San Antonio was the main overland route into East Texas. Families arriving from the east used this road to reach the interior.

key_infrastructure.name: Big Cypress Bayou / Caddo Lake navigation
key_infrastructure.impact: Steamboat access to Shreveport and New Orleans via Caddo Lake. Made Jefferson the busiest inland port in Texas and gave Harrison County a market connection no other East Texas county matched.

key_infrastructure.name: Republic of Texas land grants
key_infrastructure.impact: The Republic offered generous headright grants — 4,605 acres for heads of families who arrived before March 1836. Later grants were smaller but still substantial. These grants drew Southern families to East Texas.

major_employers.name: Subsistence and cotton farms
major_employers.employment_scale: Dominant mode — most families farmed their own land with 0-20 enslaved workers

wage_benchmarks.source: Campbell, An Empire for Slavery, ch. 2
Campbell, An Empire for Slavery, ch. 2

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} came to East Texas in the {decade}s, the land was cheap because it was far from everything — the nearest port was a steamboat ride through cypress swamp, the nearest railroad was in another state, and the nearest neighbor might be a mile through the pines.
economic_eras.source: Campbell, An Empire for Slavery, ch. 2. Jordan, Trails to Texas, ch. 3.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Cotton plantation agriculture in Harrison and Marion counties; cotton-and-corn yeoman farming elsewhere; iron furnaces in Cherokee and Cass counties; Jefferson steamboat commerce
economic_eras.description: By 1860, East Texas was divided into two economies: the plantation counties (Harrison, Marion) and the yeoman counties (Nacogdoches, Angelina, Cherokee, Smith, Rusk, Cass). Harrison County had more enslaved people than free people and was the wealthiest county in Texas. Jefferson was the second-largest city in Texas. The iron furnaces at Cherokee and Cass counties represented a small but significant industrial sector. Cotton was the cash crop across the region, but only Harrison County had the soil quality and market access to support plantation-scale operations.

county_specifics.note: 8,784 enslaved; 5,524 free. Wealthiest county in Texas. Plantation cotton on rich soil, shipped via steamboat to New Orleans.
Jefferson was the second-largest city in Texas. Steamboat commerce dominated — the town was a port, not a farm.
Nash Iron Works and Texas Iron Works operated with enslaved labor. Cotton farming on the uplands. Rusk was the county seat.
11,000+ enslaved. The second-largest enslaved population in the cluster. Tyler was a growing courthouse town.
Hughes Iron Works operated with enslaved labor. Cotton farming on sandy loam. Linden was a small courthouse town.
Mixed economy — cotton, corn, livestock. Stephen F. Austin State Teachers College site was still forest.
Moderate cotton farming on sandy loam. Henderson was a small courthouse town.
Sparsely settled. Pine forest dominated. Small subsistence farms. No significant commercial agriculture.

key_infrastructure.name: Jefferson steamboat port at peak operation
key_infrastructure.impact: Jefferson handled more tonnage than any Texas port except Galveston. Cotton, hides, and agricultural products went downstream; manufactured goods, supplies, and immigrants came upstream.

key_infrastructure.name: Nash Iron Works (Cherokee County)
key_infrastructure.impact: East Texas's first significant iron furnace. Produced bar iron for farm tools and construction. Used enslaved labor and local bog iron ore.

key_infrastructure.name: Hughes Iron Works (Cass County)
key_infrastructure.impact: Produced iron from local bog iron ore using enslaved labor and charcoal from hardwood forests.

major_employers.name: Iron furnaces (Cherokee, Cass)
major_employers.employment_scale: Dozens to hundreds of workers per furnace

major_employers.name: Steamboat commerce (Marion)
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of dockworkers, boatmen, warehouse workers

wage_benchmarks.source: Campbell, An Empire for Slavery, ch. 3
Campbell, An Empire for Slavery, ch. 3

economic_eras.narrative_hook: Harrison County in 1860 was the richest county in Texas — and the arithmetic was human. More people were enslaved there than lived free. {ancestor_name}'s world was defined by which side of that arithmetic {pronoun_subject} stood on.
economic_eras.source: Campbell, An Empire for Slavery, ch. 3. Dahmer, Caddo Was..., ch. 4.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Confederate wartime production (Marshall); postwar collapse of plantation economy; sharecropping; freedpeople's community building; Jefferson's decline begins
economic_eras.description: East Texas was strongly Confederate — unlike the German Hill Country to the west, there was no significant Unionist sentiment. Marshall served as the Confederate capital of Missouri-in-exile and a Trans-Mississippi supply and manufacturing center. The region saw no major battles but supplied men, materials, and cotton (smuggled through Mexico) to the Confederate cause. Emancipation freed over 20,000 enslaved people across the cluster — the announcement reached East Texas on June 19, 1865 (Juneteenth). Reconstruction brought sharecropping, the crop lien system, and sustained racial violence. Jefferson's decline began in the 1870s when the Great Raft was cleared from the Red River.

county_specifics.note: Marshall was the Trans-Mississippi Confederate headquarters. Emancipation freed 8,784 people. Sharecropping replaced plantation slavery on the same land.
Jefferson's steamboat port declined after the Great Raft was cleared (1870s). The railroad bypassed Jefferson for Marshall. The city shrank from 7,000 to under 3,000.
Iron furnaces continued under Confederate control during the war. Texas State Penitentiary established at Rusk in 1883, using convict labor.
Tyler served as a Confederate supply depot. Postwar: Camp Ford, the largest Confederate prisoner-of-war camp west of the Mississippi, had been located north of Tyler.
No major wartime industry. Postwar sharecropping on cotton land.
Moderate cotton sharecropping. Henderson remained a small courthouse town.
Still sparsely settled. Subsistence farming persisted.
Iron furnaces declined after the war. Cotton sharecropping became the dominant economy.

key_infrastructure.name: Marshall Confederate headquarters
key_infrastructure.impact: Marshall was the administrative center of the Confederate Trans-Mississippi Department. Powder mills, cap-and-cartridge factories, and saddleries employed hundreds.

key_infrastructure.name: Great Raft removal from Red River
key_infrastructure.impact: Army Corps cleared the massive logjam. Caddo Lake water levels dropped. Steamboat access to Jefferson ended. Jefferson's economy collapsed.

key_infrastructure.name: Texas & Pacific Railway reaches Marshall
key_infrastructure.impact: The railroad replaced the steamboat. Marshall became a railroad town; Jefferson — bypassed by the railroad — declined.

major_employers.name: Sharecropping farms
major_employers.employment_scale: Dominant employment mode for both Black and white families

major_employers.name: Texas & Pacific Railway
major_employers.employment_scale: Construction crews and permanent section gangs

wage_benchmarks.source: Moneyhon, Texas after the Civil War, ch. 3

economic_eras.narrative_hook: After Juneteenth, {ancestor_name} was free — but freedom in East Texas in 1865 meant freedom to sharecrop the same land {pronoun_subject} had worked as a slave, for a share of a crop that never quite cleared the debt at the store.
economic_eras.source: Moneyhon, Texas after the Civil War, ch. 3. Campbell, Randolph B. Gone to Texas: A History of the Lone Star State. New York: Oxford University Press, 2003.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Commercial pine lumber industry; railroad logging; sawmill company towns; cotton sharecropping continuing on cleared land
economic_eras.description: The railroad brought the timber industry to East Texas. Northern and Midwestern lumber companies — having cut out the Great Lakes forests — moved south with capital, equipment, and the capacity to strip a county of merchantable timber in a generation. Angelina County became the epicenter: the Southern Pine Lumber Company at Diboll was the prototype company town. Sawmill communities — Diboll, Keltys, Manning, Aldridge — appeared and some disappeared when the timber was cut. The timber boom transformed the landscape from virgin pine forest to cutover land. Cotton sharecropping continued alongside the timber industry but was increasingly secondary.

county_specifics.note: Timber capital of East Texas. Southern Pine Lumber Company (Diboll), Lufkin Land & Lumber Company, and numerous smaller operations. Lufkin grew from a depot to a city.
Multiple sawmill operations. Old-growth pine on the ridges was cut first, followed by loblolly on the slopes.
Smaller-scale timber operations. Jacksonville emerged as a market town.
Moderate timber operations. Henderson remained primarily agricultural.
Timber operations on the eastern portion of the county. Tyler's economy began diversifying toward commercial horticulture.
Cypress logging in the Caddo Lake swamp. Marshall was a railroad town with the T&P shops.
Moderate timber operations on remaining pine. Population began declining as timber was cut.
Small-scale timber. Jefferson continued its slow decline.

key_infrastructure.name: Houston, East & West Texas Railway reaches Lufkin
key_infrastructure.impact: Created Lufkin as a railroad town and enabled commercial timber extraction from Angelina County.

key_infrastructure.name: Southern Pine Lumber Company founded at Diboll
key_infrastructure.impact: The T.L.L. Temple family's company became the dominant employer in Angelina County. Diboll was a complete company town.

key_infrastructure.name: Logging railroad spurs (tram roads)
key_infrastructure.impact: Short-line railroads pushed into the forest from sawmill sites. Temporary communities formed along the tram roads and disappeared when the timber was cut.

major_employers.name: Numerous smaller sawmills
major_employers.employment_scale: Dozens of operations employing 10-100 workers each

wage_benchmarks.source: Maxwell and Baker, Sawdust Empire, ch. 4
Maxwell and Baker, Sawdust Empire, ch. 4
Sitton and Conrad, Nameless Towns, ch. 3

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The sawmill whistle at {town} called {ancestor_name} to work that paid more than cotton ever had — but the timber would run out, and when it did, the company town would become a ghost town and the family would have to decide what came next.
economic_eras.source: Maxwell and Baker, Sawdust Empire, ch. 4-6. Sitton and Conrad, Nameless Towns, ch. 3.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Petroleum production centered on Rusk County; oil-field service economy in Tyler; timber declining; cotton in terminal decline; Depression-era agriculture
economic_eras.description: The discovery of the East Texas Oil Field on October 3, 1930 — Dad Joiner's Daisy Bradford No. 3 well in Rusk County — transformed the region overnight. The field ultimately extended 45 miles through Rusk, Gregg, Smith, and Upshur counties, containing over 5 billion barrels of recoverable oil. Kilgore went from 700 people to 10,000 in months. Tyler became the banking and professional-services capital of the boom. The oil field produced so much oil that prices collapsed to 10 cents per barrel, forcing the Texas Railroad Commission to impose production controls (proration) that established the modern regulatory framework for American oil. For families who owned mineral rights, the boom was transformative wealth. For families who did not, it was the chaos of a boomtown without the payoff.

county_specifics.note: Epicenter. Joiner well in southern Rusk County. Kilgore boomtown in northwestern Rusk. Henderson overwhelmed.
Tyler became the commercial and banking center for the oil field. Not a major drilling county but the financial hub.
Oil field extended into western Harrison County. Marshall benefited from proximity.
Limited oil activity. Jacksonville's tomato economy was a separate story.
Limited oil activity. Economy remained timber and agriculture.
Timber remained primary. Limited oil production.
Limited oil activity. Jefferson remained in decline.
No significant oil production. Agricultural and timber economy.

key_infrastructure.name: Joiner well (Daisy Bradford No. 3)
key_infrastructure.impact: Opened the East Texas Oil Field — the largest oil field in the lower 48 states.

key_infrastructure.name: Texas Railroad Commission proration orders
key_infrastructure.impact: Production controls prevented the field from being drained in an uncontrolled rush. Established the model for American oil regulation.

key_infrastructure.name: East Texas pipeline network
key_infrastructure.impact: Pipeline construction connected the field to Gulf Coast refineries, creating permanent infrastructure.

major_employers.name: Oil drilling companies and producers
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of workers in drilling, production, and pipeline construction

major_employers.name: Oil-field service and supply companies (Tyler)
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of businesses serving the oil industry

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When Dad Joiner hit oil in Rusk County in October 1930, {ancestor_name} discovered that the land beneath the cotton rows was worth more than the crop above it — if {pronoun_subject} still owned the mineral rights.
economic_eras.source: Prindle, Petroleum Politics, ch. 2. Rundell, Early Texas Oil, ch. 8.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Stabilized oil production; managed timber (sustained-yield forestry); Tyler as regional commercial center; Angelina National Forest; out-migration from rural counties
economic_eras.description: By the late 1940s, the East Texas economy had stabilized into a managed-resource pattern. The oil field was producing under Railroad Commission proration — steady revenue rather than boom-bust chaos. The timber industry had transitioned from cut-and-run to sustained-yield forestry, with Temple Industries at Diboll leading the shift to managed pine plantations. Tyler's economy diversified around oil services, rose nurseries, and regional commercial functions. The rural counties — Cass, Marion, western Cherokee — continued losing population as families moved to Tyler, Dallas, Shreveport, and Houston for industrial wages the land could no longer provide.

county_specifics.note: Tyler emerged as the regional center — banking, retail, medical, educational. Rose nursery industry at peak. Oil services continued.
Temple Industries pioneered sustained-yield forestry at Diboll. Angelina National Forest under federal management. Lufkin diversified into foundry and manufacturing.
Oil production steady under proration. Henderson grew slowly. Kilgore settled from boomtown to oil town.
Stephen F. Austin State College (university by 1969) became the largest employer. The college payroll replaced timber and cotton.
Marshall maintained T&P railroad shops. Some oil production. Military presence at nearby Barksdale AFB (Shreveport) provided economic spillover.
Jacksonville's tomato industry peaked. Some diversification into poultry and light manufacturing.
Continued decline. Jefferson's tourist economy emerged as the town traded on its antebellum charm.
Population declining. Agricultural and timber economy insufficient to retain working-age families.

key_infrastructure.name: Temple Industries sustained-yield forestry
key_infrastructure.impact: The Temple family's decision to manage pine forests for sustained production rather than cut-and-move saved Diboll and created the model for modern Southern forestry.

key_infrastructure.name: Angelina National Forest management
key_infrastructure.impact: Federal forest management restored cutover land and provided employment through timber sales and conservation work.

key_infrastructure.name: Stephen F. Austin State College expansion
key_infrastructure.impact: College enrollment and payroll growth made SFA Nacogdoches's largest employer and economic stabilizer.

major_employers.name: Temple Industries (Diboll)
major_employers.employment_scale: 1,000+ workers in mill and forestry operations

major_employers.name: Tyler commercial sector
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands across banking, retail, medical, and oil services

major_employers.name: Stephen F. Austin State College
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred faculty and staff; student body 3,000+ by 1960

wage_benchmarks.source: Maxwell and Baker, Sawdust Empire, ch. 8

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By the {decade}s, East Texas had settled into something steadier than boom — the oil pumped under proration, the pine trees grew on managed schedules, and {ancestor_name}'s children could see that the future was in Tyler's offices or Diboll's managed forests, not in the cotton field.
economic_eras.source: Maxwell and Baker, Sawdust Empire, ch. 8. Prindle, Petroleum Politics, ch. 5. Campbell, Gone to Texas, ch. 20.

economic_turning_points.event: Cherokee expulsion — Battle of the Neches
economic_turning_points.impact: Opened northern East Texas to Anglo settlement. Cherokee improvements and cleared land were seized. Created the land base for Cherokee, Smith, and portions of surrounding counties.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The Cherokee were driven from East Texas in July 1839, and the land they left behind — cleared, fenced, planted — became the foundation for {ancestor_name}'s farm.

economic_turning_points.event: Great Raft cleared — Jefferson port dies
economic_turning_points.impact: Army Corps removal of the Red River logjam dropped Caddo Lake levels below navigable depth. Jefferson lost steamboat access and declined from the second-largest city in Texas to a fading courthouse town. Marshall, on the railroad, inherited Jefferson's commercial role.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: When the Army Corps cleared the Great Raft, the water dropped and the steamboats stopped coming to Jefferson — {ancestor_name} watched the wharves empty and the city shrink.

economic_turning_points.event: Railroad reaches Angelina County — Lufkin founded
economic_turning_points.impact: The Houston, East & West Texas Railway created Lufkin and enabled commercial timber extraction from the East Texas Piney Woods.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: Lufkin did not exist before the railroad put a depot on a pine ridge in 1882 — by the time {ancestor_name} arrived, the saws were already screaming.
economic_turning_points.source: Maxwell and Baker, Sawdust Empire, ch. 3.

economic_turning_points.event: East Texas Oil Field discovered — Joiner well
economic_turning_points.impact: The largest oil field in the lower 48 states transformed Rusk County overnight and made Tyler the financial center of the boom.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: October 3, 1930 — Dad Joiner hit oil at 3,536 feet in Rusk County, and {ancestor_name}'s cotton-and-pine world became an oil field overnight.

economic_turning_points.impact: Over 20,000 enslaved people in the eight-county cluster were freed. The plantation economy of Harrison County — the wealthiest county in Texas — was dismantled overnight. Sharecropping replaced slavery as the dominant labor arrangement.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: Juneteenth reached East Texas on June 19, 1865 — {ancestor_name} learned {pronoun_subject} was free two months after Appomattox, because freedom traveled slowly to the last corner of the Confederacy.
economic_turning_points.source: Campbell, An Empire for Slavery, ch. 8.

narrative_hooks: East Texas went through four economies in a century — cotton, timber, oil, and managed resources — and {ancestor_name} lived through the transition from one to another.

When {ancestor_name} looked for work in {county} County around {year}, the options were the cotton field, the sawmill, or leaving. Most of what the land offered had already been taken by the generation before.
The mineral rights beneath {ancestor_name}'s East Texas farm were either a fortune or a footnote — depending on whether a previous owner had separated them from the surface deed.
The sawmill whistle and the oil derrick replaced the cotton gin as the sounds of East Texas — {ancestor_name} heard the change coming before {pronoun_subject} saw it.

{ancestor_name} came to East Texas from {origin_state} because the land was cheap and the Republic of Texas was giving away headrights — the same calculus that brought thousands of Southern families into the Piney Woods.
Jefferson's wharves were the gateway to East Texas before the railroad — {ancestor_name} may have stepped off a steamboat from Shreveport onto the busiest inland port in Texas.
Harrison County in 1860 was the wealthiest county in Texas, and the wealth was measured in cotton bales and enslaved human beings — {ancestor_name} was on one side of that ledger or the other.
source_references: Campbell, Randolph B. An Empire for Slavery: The Peculiar Institution in Texas, 1821–1865. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1989.
Campbell, Randolph B. Gone to Texas: A History of the Lone Star State. New York: Oxford University Press, 2003.
Maxwell, Robert S., and Robert D. Baker. Sawdust Empire: The Texas Lumber Industry, 1830–1940. College Station: Texas A&M University Press, 1983.
Prindle, David F. Petroleum Politics and the Texas Railroad Commission. Austin: University of Texas Press, 1981.
Moneyhon, Carl H. Texas after the Civil War. College Station: Texas A&M University Press, 2004.
Sitton, Thad, and James H. Conrad. Nameless Towns: Texas Sawmill Communities, 1880–1942. Austin: University of Texas Press, 1998.
Dahmer, Fred. Caddo Was... A Short History of Caddo Lake. Austin: University of Texas Press, 1995.
Everett, Dianna. The Texas Cherokees. Norman: University of Oklahoma Press, 1990.
Jordan, Terry G. Trails to Texas. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 1981.
Rundell, Walter, Jr. Early Texas Oil. College Station: Texas A&M University Press, 1977.
